title: 4 4 Centre of Mass
body:
4.4 CENTRE OF MASS AND MOMENTS OF INTERTIA


                  The mass of a lamina Ω with density δ(x, y) is given by
                                       Z Z
                                  M=        δ(x, y)dA.
                                              Ω

            The first moment of Ω about the x-axis and y-axis respectively are
                        Z Z                         Z Z
                   Mx =       y δ(x, y)dA,    My =        x δ(x, y)dA.
                              Ω                                Ω

                              The centre of mass (x̄, ȳ) of Ω is
                   RR                                  RR
                      Ω
                        x δ(x, y) dA   M y                Ω
                                                            y δ(x, y) dA   Mx
              x̄ =                   =     ,    ȳ =                     =    .
                          M            M                      M            M
 The second moment or moment of inertia of Ω about the x-axis and y-axis respectively
                                        are
                      Z Z                        Z Z
                           2
                Ix =      y δ(x, y)dA,      Iy =      x2 δ(x, y)dA.
                              Ω                            Ω

                The polar moment of inertia about the origin is defined by
                                        Z Z
                         I0 = Ix + Iy =      (x2 + y 2 )δ(x, y)dA.
                                               Ω




The centre of mass of an object is the point where the mass is evenly balanced in all
directions. If the mass is uniformly distributed (constant density) then the centre of mass
will be the same as the centroid, the geometric centre of the object. More generally,
though, the centre of mass will not be the same as the centroid. Knowing the centre of
mass greatly simplifies calculations of the dynamics of the object because we can pretend
that all of the mass is located at that point.

In this course we will only examine the centre of mass of laminas, that is, flat sheets of
material with negligible thickness. Think of a thin tile or plate. The centre of mass (x̄, ȳ)
of such an object is the unique point where you could balance the plate on the tip of your
finger. These ideas are easily extended to objects of uniform cross-section, that is, they
vary in x and y but not in z. For example, the shaft of a rotor has a uniform cross-section.

If the density (mass per area) of the lamina is given by δ(x, y) then the mass in an
infinitesimal area dA is given by
                     mass = density × area        =⇒   dm = δ(x, y) dA.
The total mass of the object with a shape given by Ω is the integral of the density,
                                       Z Z
                                 M=         δ(x, y)dA.
                                             Ω



                                              1
 The centre of mass can be though of as the fulcrum of a lever that can rotate about
an axis. Imagine that we place an object of mass m on a lever a distance x from the
y-axis (x = 0). Then the mass would tend to rotate the lever around the y-axis. This is
measured by the first moment of mass about the y-axis,
                  first moment = distance × mass      =⇒    My = x m
The heavier the mass, the greater the first moment. But we can also increase the first
moment by moving further out along the x-axis. A long lever has a greater moment than
a short lever.

For a lamina Ω with density δ(x, y), we calculate the first moment about the y-axis by
integrating over the first moments of infinitesimal masses dm,
                                Z Z            Z Z
                           My =      x dm =         x δ(x, y) dA.
                                   Ω              Ω
The corresponding first moment about the x-axis is
                             Z Z           Z Z
                        Mx =      y dm =         y δ(x, y) dA.
                                   Ω              Ω




We can now find the location of the centre of mass. Imagine that we replace the lamina
Ω with a single point mass M . If we place this point mass at x̄, the x-coordinate of the
centre of mass, then the first moment of this point mass is the same as for the lamina Ω,
                                      Z Z
                                My =        x dm = x̄ M.
                                          Ω
A similar equation can be derived for the y-coordinate of the centre of mass. Solving
these equations gives,
                  RR                              RR
                     Ω
                       x δ(x, y) dA   My             Ω
                                                       y δ(x, y) dA   Mx
             x̄ =                   =    ,   ȳ =                   =    .
                         M            M                  M            M
                                              2
 How do we measure how hard it is to rotate an object? The resistance of an object
to acceleration is called inertia. The resistance of an object to rotations is called the
moment of inertia. For a point mass m located a distance x from the origin, the
moment of inertia for rotations about the y-axis is

                      moment of inertia = distance2 × mass = x2 m.

Heavier objects have a larger moment of inertia — they are more resistant to rotations.
But we can also increase the moment of inertia by moving the mass further from the
axis of rotation. This is how ice-skaters can control their spin by extending their arm or
bringing them closer to their bodies.

For a lamina Ω with density δ(x, y), the moments of inertia of the lamina about the x
and y axes are
                     Z Z                         Z Z
                           2
                Ix =      y δ(x, y) dA,     Iy =       x2 δ(x, y) dA.
                           Ω                              Ω

These tell us how hard it is to rotate the lamina Ω about the x and y-axes. The polar
moment of inertia tells us how hard it is to rotate Ω about the origin. It is given by
                                       Z Z
                        I0 = Ix + Iy =       (x2 + y 2 ) δ(x, y) dA.
                                             Ω

In this lecture, we will use these equations to set up the relevant double integrals, but the
actual evaluation will be left to you. In all cases, it will be important to take advantage
of symmetry to simplify the calculations as much as possible.

Example 1 A thin plate Ω is the region in the first quadrant bounded by the coordinate
axes and x + y = 1. Find the mass M and centre of mass (x̄, ȳ) of the plate assuming

a) uniform density δ(x, y) = 1.

b) non uniform density given by δ(x, y) = xy.

a) (For uniform density the centre of mass is called the centroid)

                                               M M      3 3
         1         1         1                 My Mx    2 2
F b) M = 24 , Mx = 60 , My = 60 , (x̄, ȳ) = (   ,   )=( , ) F
                                               M M      5 5

                           4
 Example 2 Find the mass, centre of mass and the moment of inertia about the y-axis
of a thin plate bounded by the curves y = x2 and y = 2x − x2 with variable density
given by δ(x, y) = x + 1.




                                                       My Mx      8 8        1
                         4

                            , My = 15 , (x̄, ȳ) = (     ,   ) = ( , ), Iy =     F
                                                       M M        15 15      6

Example 3 Find the mass and the centre of mass of a lamina bounded by y =       1 − x2
and the x axis assuming:

a) uniform density δ(x, y) = 1.

b) variable density given by δ(x, y) = y.

c) variable density given by δ(x, y) = r.




                                            6

                 F a) M = π2 , Mx = 23 , My = 0, (x̄, ȳ) = (    ,   ) = (0,    ) F
                                                               M M           3π
                                                               My Mx         3π
                  F b) M = 23 , Mx = π8 , My = 0, (x̄, ȳ) = (   ,   ) = (0,    ) F
                                                               M M           16
                                                               My Mx          3
                  F c) M = π3 , Mx = 12 , My = 0, (x̄, ȳ) = (   ,   ) = (0,    ) F
                                                               M M           2π
